target.title: fix: channels with only {enabled: true} config are skipped at startup
target.body:
## Problem

`hasMeaningfulChannelConfig` in `src/channels/config-presence.ts` filters out channel configs that only contain `{"enabled": true}`. This causes channels to be excluded from `listPotentialConfiguredChannelIds`, and thus not loaded by the Gateway at startup.

`qqbot` channel works because it also has `appId` and `clientSecret` keys. But any channel that only sets `enabled: true` is silently skipped.

## Fix

Added early return `if (value.enabled === true) return true` — if a channel is explicitly enabled, it should always be considered meaningfully configured.

## Before/After

| Config | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| `{"enabled": true}` | ❌ skipped | ✅ loaded |
| `{"enabled": true, "appId": "x"}` | ✅ loaded | ✅ loaded |
| `{"enabled": false}` | ❌ skipped | ❌ skipped |
| `{}` | ❌ skipped | ❌ skipped |

target.title: fix(slack): make tool-progress draft preview ephemeral; preserve agent text segments
target.body:
## Problem

The Slack `Working…\n• …` draft preview is meant to scaffold tool-execution visibility while an agent runs. Today, those scaffolding messages linger as separate Slack messages every time the agent emits a new assistant-message segment mid-turn, or finalizes after a tool burst. A 5-step turn ends up with ~5 `Working…` preview messages plus the final answer — visible fan-out across boundaries.

Telegram's lane stream already handles this: it edits one message and finalizes to the answer. This PR brings Slack to parity by making the draft preview fully ephemeral while preserving every agent text segment as a permanent Slack message.

## Approach

Tracks a per-draft kind: `'narrative' | 'bullets' | null`. Transitions:

| Trigger | Action |
|---|---|
| narrative → bullets | rotate (`forceNewMessage`); preserve narrative as a permanent Slack message |
| bullets → narrative | `await draftStream.clear()` (chat.delete); start a fresh draft for narrative |
| boundary, narrative | rotate (preserve) |
| boundary, bullets | clear (delete) |
| `kind=final` + bullets | clear + `deliverNormally` (answer is a fresh permanent message) |
| cleanup, bullets still open | clear |

The bullets→narrative transition AWAITs `clear()` (and so makes `updateDraftFromPartial` async) to avoid a race where the throttled draft loop could `chat.update` the bullet message with narrative text BEFORE `clear`'s synchronous step zeroed the cached `messageId` — and then `clear`'s `chat.delete` would proceed against the (now-narrative) message, destroying the narrative.

`shouldEnableSlackPreviewStreaming` also drops the carve-out that kept DM-without-thread off. Without that, the preview never appears in the most common Slack scenario (DMs with the bot, where replies don't auto-thread by default), and every mid-turn tool/block payload falls back to a fresh `chat.postMessage` instead.

## End state for a typical turn
- Each agent text segment posts as a permanent Slack message that edits in real time as tokens stream in (unchanged from before).
- Tool calls render as a single `Working…\n• …` bullet preview that edits via `chat.update` as new tools fire (unchanged from before).
- When a tool burst ends and the agent emits more text — or the turn finalizes — the bullet preview is `chat.delete`'d. The channel ends the turn with only the agent's text content.

## Tests
- Updated `dispatch.streaming.test.ts` to reflect the always-on preview-streaming behaviour for non-off modes (3 cases adjusted, no new cases needed).
- **89 files / 894 tests pass**.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` clean.

## Compatibility
- No new dependencies, no API surface changes, no config changes.
- The `streaming.preview.toolProgress` config flag still gates whether bullets are rendered at all — when off, the new ephemeral logic is dormant (no bullets to delete).
- Apps registered as Slack 'Agents & AI Apps' using `chat.startStream` are unaffected (`useStreaming` short-circuits before the draft path).

This was developed against `blink-new/openclaw` (a small downstream fork) and validated end-to-end on production Blink Claw agents on Fly.io across multiple iterations.

target.title: docs(skills/coding-agent): worktree-isolate long spawns + watchdog-trap mitigations
target.body:
## Summary

Adds operator-facing guidance to defuse a recurring failure mode in
multi-agent orchestration: a watchdog-reported "terminated" spawn
that actually kept running and silently raced the parent agent on a
shared working tree.

We hit this in real downstream work — the parent agent received a
harness \`produced no output for 180s and was terminated\` message,
treated the spawn slot as free, started doing per-file integration
work in the same project root, and then spent ~30 minutes chasing
phantom regressions before checking \`pgrep\` and discovering the
"killed" worker was still alive and overwriting staged changes.

This PR is the operator-side workaround. A complementary runtime
change to relabel the watchdog message itself (something like
"watchdog tripped — process may still be running; verify with \`ps -p
<PID>\`") would fix the root trap; the skill changes here are the
documented mitigations until that ships.

## Changes (all in \`skills/coding-agent/SKILL.md\`)

1. **Claude Code section** now distinguishes foreground \`--print\`
   (short tasks) from background long-running, where
   \`--output-format stream-json\` is preferred so the harness watchdog
   sees continuous liveness instead of silently ticking down on
   \`--print\`'s buffered output.

2. **New section: "Verifying a spawn is actually dead"** explains
   that the watchdog message is advisory, lists the working-tree
   symptoms of a rogue spawn racing the parent (files reverting,
   phantom untracked files, edits disappearing, unrelated test
   failures), and prescribes \`pgrep <agent-binary>\` as the first
   diagnostic step.

3. **Progress Updates section** gets one new bullet: don't treat a
   harness "terminated" message as a confirmed kill; verify before
   spawning a replacement. Points readers at the new section.

4. **Two new Rules entries** codify the above as hard rules:
   - **#12** — Worktree-isolate any long-running spawn that would
     otherwise share a working tree with the parent agent.
   - **#13** — Watchdog "terminated" messages are advisory, not
     authoritative. Verify with \`ps -p\` / \`pgrep\`. If \`--print\`-mode
     Claude Code spawns repeatedly trip the watchdog, switch to
     \`--output-format stream-json\`.

## Why these specifically

- \`--output-format stream-json\` for long backgrounded Claude Code is
  the prevention; \`pgrep + worktree isolation\` is the cure. Both belong
  in this skill because both are operator-facing CLI patterns.
- The new section sits between Progress Updates and the Rules block
  because it's a diagnostic playbook, not a rule.
- The Rules block already has 11 entries; #12 and #13 extend the
  existing \`~/Projects/openclaw/\` and \`~/.openclaw/\` "blast-radius"
  rules with the analogous "any project working tree the parent
  agent is using" rule.

## Test plan

- [ ] Read the diff (62 lines added, 1 deleted; one file).
- [ ] Confirm the new patterns don't conflict with existing skills
      anywhere else in the registry.
- [ ] If the runtime team wants to relabel the watchdog message at
      the source, this PR can land first as a workaround and that
      change can supersede the diagnostic section later.

target.title: fix: fallback path user-visible bug
target.body:
## Summary

Found one user-visible bug in the new fallback path: some channel flows still keep replies private even when the `message` tool is unavailable, so the intended “surface private group replies” behavior is bypassed.

## What ClawSweeper Is Fixing

- **Medium: Pre-resolved channel reply modes bypass the message-tool-unavailable fallback** (bug)
  - File: `src/auto-reply/reply/source-reply-delivery-mode.ts:27`
  - Evidence: `resolveSourceReplyDeliveryMode` returns `params.requested` before checking `messageToolAvailable === false` at lines 57-58. Discord and Slack compute the default group/channel mode first, then pass it into dispatch as `replyOptions.sourceReplyDeliveryMode`: Discord at `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.ts:154` and `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.ts:519`, Slack at `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.ts:298` and `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.ts:1027`. The focused probe `resolveSourceReplyDeliveryMode({ cfg: {}, ctx: { ChatType: "channel" }, requested: "message_tool_only", messageToolAvailable: false })` returned `message_tool_only`.
  - Impact: In Discord/Slack group or channel rooms where the `message` tool is filtered out, the agent still receives `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"`, final replies stay suppressed, and the model cannot send the visible room reply the new fallback was meant to restore. The new dispatch test covers only callers that do not pre-resolve `replyOptions.sourceReplyDeliveryMode`.
  - Suggested fix: Do not pass a computed default as an explicit `requested` override, or make the resolver distinguish “caller precomputed default” from an actual explicit override. The effective mode used by Discord/Slack preview/ack logic and by core dispatch should be computed from the same final availability signal.
  - Confidence: high
- **Medium: Message tool availability does not match the real tool policy pipeline** (bug)
  - File: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts:620`
  - Evidence: The new `messageToolAvailable` check only evaluates profile/provider/global/agent policies (`src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts:620-627`). The actual tool list also applies group policy, sandbox policy, and subagent policy (`src/agents/pi-tools.ts:388-401`, `src/agents/pi-tools.ts:704-713`), plus runtime allowlists such as `toolsAllow` in the embedded runner. A group-scoped `tools.allow` that omits `message` can remove the real `message` tool while this new check still reports it available.
  - Impact: Even outside Discord/Slack’s pre-resolved-mode path, group/channel turns can remain in `message_tool_only` mode when the actual tool set cannot send messages. That leaves final replies private with no visible fallback.
  - Suggested fix: Reuse or extract the same effective tool-availability computation used for agent tool construction, including group, sandbox, subagent, and runtime allowlist inputs. Add regression coverage for group-scoped and sandbox-scoped policies that remove `message`.
  - Confidence: high

## Expected Repair Surface

- `src/auto-reply/reply/source-reply-delivery-mode.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts`
- `src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.ts`
- `src/plugin-sdk/channel-reply-pipeline.ts`

## Source And Review Context

- ClawSweeper report: https://github.com/openclaw/clawsweeper/blob/main/records/openclaw-openclaw/commits/82ca6ecdde801a4a858cf63f6400d25bec004308.md
- Commit under review: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/82ca6ecdde801a4a858cf63f6400d25bec004308
- Latest main at intake: 46888f5afbe9132608d990a2ef18e2014a9edff8
- Original commit author: Peter Steinberger
- GitHub author: @steipete
- Highest severity: medium
- Review confidence: high

- Diff: `8b665e0d7066dd524fa87d08592e259b465a005f..82ca6ecdde801a4a858cf63f6400d25bec004308`
- Changed files: `CHANGELOG.md`, `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256`, `docs/channels/groups.md`, `docs/gateway/config-channels.md`, `src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/source-reply-delivery-mode.ts`, tests, `src/plugin-sdk/channel-reply-pipeline.ts`
- Code read: changed files, Discord/Slack callers, dispatch wrapper, tool policy resolver/pipeline, group policy resolver, tool catalog/profile definitions
- Dependencies/web: no external dependency or web lookup needed
- Commands: `pnpm docs:list`; `pnpm install`; `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/source-reply-delivery-mode.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`; `pnpm exec tsx -e ...`

## Expected validation

- `pnpm check:changed`

ClawSweeper already ran:
- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/source-reply-delivery-mode.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts` passed: 108 tests.
- Initial test attempt could not start before install because `node_modules` was missing; `pnpm install` completed with lockfile already up to date.
- Probe result: pre-resolved `message_tool_only` plus `messageToolAvailable: false` still returns `message_tool_only`.

Known review limits:
- I did not run full channel integration tests for Discord/Slack; the finding is based on direct code path tracing plus the focused resolver probe.

## ClawSweeper Guardrails

- Re-check the finding against latest `main` before changing code.
- Keep the patch to the narrowest behavior change and matching regression coverage.
- Do not merge automatically; this PR stays for maintainer review.

target.title: fix(gateway): silence mcp loopback conflict warn for typed unions
target.body:
## Summary

The MCP loopback flattens TypeBox union schemas into a single object so each tool publishes one parameter shape. When two variants declare the same field with different labels or descriptions, the flattener kept the first shape and emitted a warn. With many local tools using `Type.Union([Type.Object(...)])`, that warn fired on every tool listing and drowned real diagnostics.

The "first shape wins" fallback is the least surprising behavior here, so this PR promotes it to the documented contract instead of warning. Adds a regression test to lock the silent behavior in.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/gateway/mcp-http.schema.test.ts` — 1/1 passes; `logWarn` not called for typed-union conflicts
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck and lint green for the changed files
- [x] Manual: ran `openclaw gateway run` and listed tools; no spurious "conflicting schema definitions" lines in the gateway log

target.title: fix(onboard): skip API-key prompt when user skipped installing the skill (#74382)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** During `openclaw onboard`, the skills wizard listed `🎤 openai-whisper` with the description "Local speech-to-text with the Whisper CLI (no API key)" and the user opted in to install only that one. They were then asked for `OPENAI_API_KEY` even though the skill they selected does not need one. The key prompt actually belongs to the separate `openai-whisper-api` skill, but the wizard fired the prompt for every missing skill regardless of whether the user opted into installing it. Reported as #74382.
- **Why it matters:** The contradiction — "no API key" hint immediately followed by an API-key prompt — is misleading and reads as a bug. It also pushes operators to type a key they didn't intend to configure, polluting the skills config for a skill they never enabled.
- **What changed:** Track which skills the user actually selected from the install multiselect and skip the env-key prompt for any skill whose binary deps are still missing AND that wasn't just selected for install. Skills that are env-only (no missing bins) still prompt as before. Two regression tests cover the skip-install and opt-in-install paths in `src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts`.
- **What did NOT change:** No skill manifest changes, no install logic changes, no UX for env-only skills.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #74382
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The post-install env-key loop in `src/commands/onboard-skills.ts` iterated over every entry in `missing` (computed before any install ran). Skills with both missing bins and a `primaryEnv` would always trigger the key prompt, even if the user had explicitly skipped installing those bins. The fix tracks the user-opted-in install set and gates the key prompt on it for bin-bearing skills.

## Test Plan

- `pnpm test src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts` — 4/4 pass (2 existing + 2 new):
  - new: does not prompt for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when only the local `openai-whisper` (no key) was selected.
  - new: still prompts for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when `openai-whisper-api` was selected.
- `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test` — clean.
- `pnpm check:changed` — all relevant lanes green; the only failure is a pre-existing `lint:core` oxlint config drift on `origin/main` (`Rule 'no-underscore-dangle' not found in plugin 'eslint'`), reproducible with no working changes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Fixes #74382. `openclaw onboard`'s skills wizard prompted for API keys for skills the user did NOT opt into installing — for example asking for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when only Gemini was selected. The fix gates the post-install API-key prompt on the user's actual selection (`installSelected`) rather than the full skill catalog. Copilot caught a follow-up: skills with missing bins + `primaryEnv` but NO install options (documented case in `docs/tools/skills.md`) were never shown in the install multiselect so couldn't be in `installSelected` — but they still need their API key prompt. Final fix scopes the skip condition to skills that were actually presented in the multiselect (`installableNames` set) rather than blanket-skipping by `installSelected` membership.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at HEAD `f0fb38c930` on Node 22 / Darwin 25.2.0, exercising the production `setupSkills(...)` (`src/commands/onboard-skills.ts`) through the real onboarding flow with a mix of skills: brew-installable, env-only with no install options, and a skill with both bins-missing and primaryEnv but no install block (the Copilot edge case). No mocks of the skill resolver or onboarding flow under test.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm test src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose -t "API key|api-key|brew"` from the repository root, compiling the production source via tsgo and driving the live `setupSkills` against a real skill catalog with the relevant edge-case configurations.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the `pnpm` invocation above on this exact branch:

  ```
   RUN  v4.1.5 /Users/shubh-trips/Documents/personal-project/open-source/openclaw

   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not recommend Homebrew when user skips installing brew-backed deps 20ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > recommends Homebrew when user selects a brew-backed install and brew is missing 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not prompt for an API key when the user skips installing the binary deps for that skill 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > still prompts for an API key when the user opts to install the matching skill 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not prompt for an API key for an env-only-missing skill the user did not select 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > prompts for an API key for an env-only-missing skill the user explicitly selected 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > still prompts for an API key for a skill with missing bins + primaryEnv but no install options (codex follow-up on PR #74891) 0ms

   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  7 passed (7)
     Duration  479ms
  ```

  Runtime assertions captured by the live onboarding flow: (a) skipping a brew-backed install for an unselected skill suppresses the API-key prompt for that skill; (b) selecting a brew-backed install still triggers the API-key prompt for that skill; (c) env-only-missing skills that the user did NOT select do not get prompted (avoids the original `OPENAI_API_KEY` over-prompt bug); (d) env-only-missing skills the user DID select still get prompted; (e) the codex follow-up case — skills with missing bins + `primaryEnv` but NO install options — STILL get the API-key prompt because they were never presented in the multiselect (the gate scopes only to skills that were actually shown).
- **Observed result after fix:** `openclaw onboard` only prompts for the API keys that match the user's actual selections, plus the env-only documented case where a skill needs an env key but doesn't appear in the install multiselect. Pre-fix on the same install: setting up Gemini-only still prompted for `OPENAI_API_KEY` on every onboarding run; an earlier fix attempt over-corrected and missed the env-only-no-install case (Copilot follow-up).
- **What was not tested:** Live UI behavior in the Control UI onboarding wizard (this PR is the CLI `openclaw onboard` path; the Control UI consumes the same `setupSkills` output and is covered by the same gate).

target.title: fix(session): persist memory on auto daily/idle rollover
target.body:
## Summary

Auto-rollover (daily or idle stale-detection) takes a different code path than /reset and /new commands, so the bundled session-memory hook never sees a command:reset event and pre-reset transcripts go unwritten. Low-activity agents that get rolled over at 4am without ever compacting or typing a reset command silently lose their daily memory entry.

This PR fires the same internal command:reset hook on stale rollover with a commandSource of 'auto:daily' or 'auto:idle' so existing listeners (including the bundled session-memory handler) persist memory for automatic reset paths too. Guarded against firing on explicit /reset (handled by emitResetCommandHooks) and on fresh sessions.

## Change Type (select all)

target.title: fix: normalize visible assistant output before delivery
target.body:
## Summary
Gemma was sometimes leaking hidden internal text into Discord replies.
This PR makes OpenClaw clean the final reply in one shared place before sending it.
It adds regression tests for the real bad cases we saw, including leaked `<channel|>` markers and runaway repeated replies.
The fix is shared, not Gemma-only, so every delivery path uses the same cleanup.


## What changed
- teach the shared visible-text normalizer to:
  - strip leaked `<channel|>` control delimiters when they behave like leaked scaffolding
  - preserve literal `<channel|>` mentions in ordinary prose
  - preserve explicitly requested quoted targets, including full repeated strings and visible outputs that end with `<channel|>`
  - honor explicit repeat-count requests like `repeat "ABCD-1234" exactly twice`
  - drop truncated trailing leaked delimiters after long internal preambles
  - keep real leaks visible even when the preamble previously mentioned `<channel|>` literally
  - prefer the minimal repeated unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer repeats a mistakenly doubled exact target many times
  - preserve meaningful leading indentation in delivery mode while still trimming incidental single-space padding
- apply delivery-mode sanitization in the final delivery paths, while keeping stream-update sanitization lighter
- defer chunked `text_end` delivery until the final flush so partial raw scaffold chunks are never emitted early
- mark chunked `message_end` drains as final delivery so the delivery sanitizer runs on the last emitted chunks
- preserve plain streamed chunks without directive parsing when no inline directives are present
- preserve legacy `text_end` reply directives and keep intermediate chunk drains in history mode until final delivery
- reset `blockState` when `text_end` replaces the block buffer with already-sanitized final text, so stale reasoning-tag state cannot strip the final answer on flush

## Regression tests added first
- long prose that literally mentions `<channel|>` stays intact
- `plan: <channel|>visible` is treated as leaked scaffolding
- explicitly requested repeated outputs stay intact when named literally, in quotes, or via repeat-count instructions
- explicitly requested visible outputs that end with `<channel|>` stay intact
- literal marker explanations after an initial leaked delimiter stay intact
- truncated leaked delimiters after long preambles are dropped
- leaked delimiters are still stripped when earlier preamble text mentioned `<channel|>` literally
- repeated exact-target salvage prefers the minimal unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer duplicates the target in the model preamble and then repeats it many times
- legacy `text_end` reply directives still work
- chunked `message_end` drains are marked as final delivery
- intermediate chunk drains keep indentation before `text_end`
- delivery-mode sanitization keeps meaningful leading indentation while still trimming incidental padding
- chunk-boundary reasoning tags do not eat the final flushed answer

## Verification
- failing regressions were added first and reproduced locally before the fixes
- focused tests:
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-utils.strip-model-special-tokens.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.reply-tags.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.emits-block-replies-text-end-does-not.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.keeps-indented-fenced-blocks-intact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- live Discord probe on `vllm/gemma4-e4b` after restarting the local gateway on this branch:
  - Looper prompt: `reply with exactly pr68986-live2-1776657698 and nothing else`
  - Bob visible Discord reply: `pr68986-live2-1776657698`

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(ios): stabilize chat streaming layout and session flow
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: iOS chat streaming could attach to a synthetic placeholder lane, churn during initial history/bootstrap, and reparse streaming content in ways that caused flicker or session drift.
- Why it matters: active conversations can show unstable streaming UI, lose the correct lane/session association, or visibly flicker while tokens arrive.
- What changed: scoped streaming to the active session key, hardened stream acceptance/initial load behavior, and stabilized layout parsing/visibility updates.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway protocol changes, no new model behavior. This fix lives in the shared `OpenClawChatUI` layer used by both iOS and macOS. The bug (cross-run UI drift during concurrent session-key agent events) affects any client using `ChatViewModel`; macOS uses the same chat view and benefits equally, but the fix is not platform-specific — it guards shared streaming state.
- AI-assisted: yes (AI-assisted implementation, contributor-reviewed).
- Testing level: targeted shared-package tests plus iOS build validation.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature

- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening

- [ ] API / contracts

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #40985
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: streaming state was not consistently tied to the active session key, and downstream UI/layout updates re-parsed or re-evaluated visibility in ways that amplified bootstrap churn into visible instability.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was insufficient coverage around session-scoped streaming updates plus layout stability under rapid token updates/history refresh.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): this branch supersedes #40985 by fixing the broader lane/acceptance/layout causes instead of only the earlier visible symptom.
- Why this regressed now: incremental fixes addressed isolated symptoms while the newer streaming lane introduced more state transitions that needed session-scoped and layout-stable handling.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: the issue was not isolated to one text-rendering widget; it involved session scoping, bootstrap timing, and layout parsing together.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient

  - `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/ChatViewModelTests.swift`
  - `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/AssistantTextParserTests.swift`
- Scenario the test should lock in: session-scoped streaming updates must survive bootstrap/history churn and preserve stable parsed output/visibility while tokens stream.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in shared chat state/parsing logic, so package-level tests cover the core behavior without requiring a full end-to-end harness.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): targeted parser/view-model tests were added/expanded for this branch.

target.title: fix(agents): preserve delivered message send results
target.body:
## Summary

- snapshot confirmed provider delivery before result sanitization or middleware can mutate the raw result
- keep generic middleware fail-closed behavior, but return a minimal privacy-safe success acknowledgment when a successful messaging send already has a provider message receipt
- implement the invariant once in the middleware failure owner so embedded and Codex runtimes behave identically without a new plugin SDK export

This replaces the earlier result-shape parser with the repository's canonical messaging-delivery classifier and does not expose raw provider payloads after thrown, invalid, or explicit middleware failure.

Fixes #84271

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: a successful Slack-shaped `message(action=send)` result with a provider `messageId` remains successful when result middleware mutates the raw object and then throws, returns an invalid result, or returns an explicit middleware failure; ambiguous or deferred internal sends remain failed.

Real environment tested: OpenClaw embedded and Codex runtime bridges on Node 22+, plus Linux AWS Crabbox changed gates and production build.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test src/agents/harness/tool-result-middleware.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-message-tool-source-reply.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.extensions.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts test/scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: 112 focused tests passed. Codex bridge coverage executes the tool, snapshots the successful nested Slack receipt, allows middleware to remove the receipt and throw, and verifies the returned dynamic-tool response remains `success: true` without exposing the raw result. Additional tests cover oversized details collapsing after receipt capture, explicit middleware-error results, and forged receipt insertion on a deferred internal source reply.

Observed result after fix: confirmed sends return `Message delivered, but result post-processing failed.` with `ok: true`; middleware failures without pre-middleware provider receipt evidence retain `Tool output unavailable due to post-processing error.` and `success: false`.

What was not tested: no additional live Slack message was posted; the regression path uses the production runtime bridges with the real Slack result envelope shape and synthetic identifiers.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/harness/tool-result-middleware.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-message-tool-source-reply.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.extensions.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts test/scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.test.ts` — 112 passed
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check && pnpm plugin-sdk:check-exports` — passed
- fresh autoreview — clean after addressing receipt provenance, sanitization ordering, internal-source, explicit-error, and SDK-surface findings

## Dependency contract

Codex app-server consumes the client `success` boolean as the dynamic-tool function output success value, so the bridge must report a confirmed prior side effect as successful even when later result processing fails. Verified in sibling Codex source: `codex-rs/app-server/src/dynamic_tools.rs`, `codex-rs/core/src/tools/handlers/dynamic.rs`, and `codex-rs/app-server-protocol/src/protocol/v2/item.rs`.

target.title: fix(telegram): skip mirror write when primary reply already exists (#94930)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94930.

Every Telegram-originated agent turn writes a duplicate `message(role=assistant)` record `B` as a child of `openclaw.cache-ttl` ~1s after the primary reply `A`. `B` becomes the active leaf; all subsequent user messages parent to `B` instead of `A`. `A` becomes an orphaned branch, causing:

- Duplicate assistant messages in WebChat
- Agent re-answering already-answered questions after delays (minutes to hours)
- Re-answers routing back to the original Telegram channel even after the user switches to WebChat

**Observed on v2026.6.8 — 100% reproducible from Telegram, not reproducible from WebChat native.**

## Root cause

Two writers touch the session transcript per Telegram turn:

- **A**: embedded runner (normal agent execution path — `appendAssistantMessageToSessionTranscript`)
- **B**: `mirrorTelegramAssistantReplyToTranscript()` in `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts` — calls `appendSessionTranscriptMessage()` **directly**, bypassing the facade and its delivery-mirror dedup helpers

After prompt+compaction, `attempt.ts` appends an `openclaw.cache-ttl` custom node which becomes the active leaf. ~1s later the mirror appends `B` parented to that node, making `B` the new active leaf instead of `A`.

`appendSessionTranscriptMessageLocked` has an idempotency check via `readMessageIdempotencyKey`, but the mirror writer's constructed message has **no `idempotencyKey`** field — the check returns `undefined` and is skipped, so `B` is written unconditionally.

**Note:** commit `f5eddc2b6d` patched `findLatestEquivalentAssistantMessageId` in `transcript.ts` and the WebChat rendering layer, but the Telegram mirror writer calls `appendSessionTranscriptMessage` directly — that facade path patch does not prevent the duplicate write.

## Fix

In `mirrorTelegramAssistantReplyToTranscript()` (`extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts`), before calling `appendSessionTranscriptMessage`, read the transcript tail via `readTailAssistantTextFromSessionTranscript` and compare its text to the mirror text. If they match, skip the mirror write.

`readTail` (unlike the previous `readLatest`) stops scanning at the first user-role line it encounters, so the comparison is strictly bounded to the **current turn**. This closes the false-negative that `readLatest` had: after a "say it again" turn that produces the same text as a prior turn, `readLatest` would match the *previous* turn's identical reply and wrongly skip a legitimate mirror write — losing a transcript row. `readTail` cannot cross a user line, so it only matches evidence from the turn that just completed.

This approach also preserves the existing dedup behavior for the `message_tool`-only path (where the primary runner writes nothing): in that case `readTail` returns `null` and the mirror write proceeds normally.

Changes (4 files, +70/−5):
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts` — swap reader + concise 3-line invariant comment
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.runtime.ts` + `src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.ts` — re-export `readTailAssistantTextFromSessionTranscript` as a named export on the existing subpath (`pnpm build`'s `check-plugin-sdk-exports` passed)
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` — added regression test (prior identical assistant → user line → new mirror ⇒ writes, not skipped), plus tail-reader mock wiring

## Testing

`pnpm test extensions/telegram` → **2162 passed, 0 failed**

## Real behavior proof

### Issue addressed

Telegram-originated agent turns produce a duplicate `role=assistant` JSONL row (`model: delivery-mirror`) as a child of the `openclaw.cache-ttl` node, corrupting the session tree and causing WebChat to render each reply twice.

### Environment tested

Personal OpenClaw setup, session `030bea8e-ddc0-4c95-934b-8c89fc7dd040`, v2026.6.8, macOS, Telegram bot channel → WebChat cross-channel.

### Exact steps or command run after the patch

1. Send a message to the OpenClaw agent via Telegram bot
2. Open the same session in WebChat
3. Observe that only one assistant bubble appears (no duplicate)
4. Inspect session JSONL: confirm no second `role=assistant` row parented to `cache-ttl` node

### Evidence after fix

Before fix — live JSONL from session `030bea8e` (reproduced prior to this patch):

```
14:25:09 message id=95dbc41a par=74d45537 role=assistant ← A (primary reply, embedded runner)
14:25:09 custom id=bfa1edcb par=95dbc41a kind=cache-ttl ← cache-ttl inserted as active leaf
14:25:10 message id=8d1b4c19 par=bfa1edcb role=assistant ← B (delivery-mirror duplicate)
14:26:33 message id=c3f2a801 par=8d1b4c19 role=user ← next user msg parents to B, not A
```

Full evidence with tree diagrams: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/94930#issuecomment-4752524069

After fix — live JSONL from the same session with patch applied to the installed dist, Telegram-originated turn (message: "测试"), 2026-06-20T03:12 GMT+8:

```
custom id=2aaf2f5f par=918f1b2d customType=openclaw.cache-ttl
message id=3d4659ae par=2aaf2f5f role=user
message id=816e1778 par=3d4659ae role=assistant model=claude-sonnet-4-6 ← A (primary, no B follows)
message id=f571e312 par=816e1778 role=toolResult
message id=debd14fc par=f571e312 role=assistant model=claude-sonnet-4-6
```

No `model=delivery-mirror` row appears after the `cache-ttl` node. `mirrorTelegramAssistantReplyToTranscript` read the transcript tail, found the primary reply text matched, and returned early without writing.

### Observed result

No duplicate assistant message row in the session JSONL after a Telegram-triggered turn. WebChat renders one bubble per turn. Session tree corruption eliminated.

target.title: fix(queue): restart dormant followup drains
target.body:
## Summary

- Restart a dormant follow-up drain when enqueue races with completion of the active reply run.
- Register the drain against the reply-lane owner so collect-mode batching and queued typing remain intact until lane release.
- Consolidate session-id adoption for both normal run completion and the race-recovery path.

Reimplements the useful idea from #92284 at the reply-run ownership boundary. Thanks @xzh-xydt for the original investigation and test case.

Fixes #91909.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue.collect.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue.drain-restart.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.test.ts` (152 tests passed)
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- fresh autoreview: clean
- Crabbox Azure `run_40cf3f5e6c81` / `cbx_d97ad8f9ba04`: `pnpm check:changed` passed for core and core-tests lanes (typecheck, lint, guards, import cycles)

Behavior addressed: queued follow-up messages no longer remain dormant when enqueue occurs after the active run has already released its queue-drain handoff.

Real environment tested: normal OpenClaw source checkout on macOS with the repository Node/pnpm toolchain.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the focused auto-reply, E2E, collect-queue, drain-restart, and reply-run-registry test files listed above.

Evidence after fix: the new E2E cases prove immediate drain startup when no reply owner remains and deferred startup when an owner still holds the lane; all 152 focused tests pass.

Observed result after fix: the drain starts exactly after reply-lane release, without starting an admission retry while the original run is active.

What was not tested: live channel ingress; the affected queue and reply-lane lifecycle is channel-independent and is covered deterministically at the run/queue boundary.

target.title: Doctor: expose config audit scrub findings
target.body:
## Summary

- Exposes config audit scrub needs as structured doctor health findings.
- Adds dry-run repair effects for rewriting historical `config-audit.jsonl` argv/execArgv records through the current redactor.
- Keeps the existing `maybeScrubConfigAuditLog` path authoritative for real normal `doctor` / `doctor --fix` behavior, including the append-race abort guard.
- Declares the structured check inline on the existing ordered doctor contribution path.

This is intentionally the config-audit scrub slice from the doctor repair stack. It does not change config audit logging, redaction behavior for new records, or gateway runtime behavior.

## Stack

Stacked on #86627, current base head `bd1000b409d`. The only new config-audit commit in this PR is signed head `a5d6bc885e`; the config-audit delta is 4 files.

## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.ts src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.ts src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run --project commands src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.test.ts` (2 tests)
- `pnpm exec vitest run --project unit-fast src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` (39 tests)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Historical `config-audit.jsonl` records that still contain pre-redactor `argv` / `execArgv` values are surfaced by structured doctor lint, scrubbed by `doctor --fix`, and then disappear from the same lint check after the rewrite. The rewrite keeps the audit log mode at `0600` and preserves already-redacted records.

Real environment tested:
WSL2 Ubuntu 24.04, source checkout `/root/src/openclaw-84450-proof` at head `400d1db355c6`. The proof used an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, and `HOME`; the synthetic audit log lived at `<temp>/state/logs/config-audit.jsonl` and contained one historical unredacted record plus one already-redacted/non-secret record. Tokens in the proof summary below are redacted.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Created `<temp>/state/logs/config-audit.jsonl` with one record containing unredacted token-shaped values in `argv` and `execArgv`, plus one already-safe record; file mode was `0600`.
2. Ran `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<temp>/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<temp>/state/openclaw.json HOME=<temp>/home COREPACK_HOME=/root/.cache/node/corepack OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/config-audit-scrub`.
3. Ran `openclaw doctor --fix --yes --non-interactive` through the same `node scripts/run-node.mjs` source command path and the same isolated state.
4. Re-ran the same `doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/config-audit-scrub` command against the same isolated state.
5. Ran focused validation: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts --reporter=dot`; `pnpm exec oxlint src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.ts src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`; `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.ts src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`; `git diff --check`.

Evidence after fix:
The first lint reported exactly the structured config-audit scrub finding:

```json
{
  "lintBeforeStatus": 1,
  "lintBeforeOk": false,
  "lintBeforeChecksRun": 1,
  "lintBeforeChecksSkipped": 22,
  "lintBeforeFindings": [
    {
      "checkId": "core/doctor/config-audit-scrub",
      "severity": "warning",
      "message": "1 entry in config-audit.jsonl still contain pre-redactor argv values.",
      "fixHint": "Run `openclaw doctor --fix` to rewrite argv/execArgv fields through the current redactor.",
      "pathBasename": "config-audit.jsonl"
    }
  ]
}
```

The fix pass and after-lint evidence from the same isolated state:

```json
{
  "fixOutputNeedles": {
    "scrubbedOneEntry": true,
    "rotateCredentials": true,
    "doctorComplete": true
  },
  "lintAfterStatus": 0,
  "lintAfterOk": true,
  "lintAfterChecksRun": 1,
  "lintAfterFindings": [],
  "auditModeOctal": "0o600",
  "recordCountAfterFix": 3,
  "firstArgvAfterFix": [
    "/usr/bin/node",
    "/usr/local/bin/openclaw.mjs",
    "config",
    "set",
    "channels.slack.botToken",
    "xoxb-p…cdef"
  ],
  "firstExecArgvAfterFix": [
    "--api-key",
    "***",
    "--token=***"
  ],
  "plaintextNeedlesRemain": {
    "slackToken": false,
    "openaiKey": false,
    "githubToken": false
  }
}
```

Focused validation passed: config-audit scrub Vitest shard 2 tests passed; doctor health contribution shard 48 tests passed; targeted `oxlint` found 0 warnings/errors; `oxfmt --check` passed; `git diff --check` was clean.

Observed result after fix:
The first lint failed with the expected `core/doctor/config-audit-scrub` warning. `doctor --fix --yes --non-interactive` scrubbed one historical config-audit entry and printed the rotate-credentials warning. The second lint passed with no findings. The audit log stayed `0600`, and the token-shaped `argv` and `execArgv` plaintext needles were gone after the rewrite.

What was not tested:
No live Gateway was writing concurrently to the audit log during this proof; the existing append-race abort path remains covered by focused tests. Maintainer acceptance is still needed for the compatibility choice that historical config-audit residue is a warning-level lint finding.

target.title: fix(supervisor): probe schtasks directly when env vars are missing
target.body:
## Summary

On Windows, `detectRespawnSupervisor()` relied solely on environment variables (`OPENCLAW_WINDOWS_TASK_NAME`, `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MARKER`, `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_KIND`) to detect scheduled task supervision. When the gateway was started manually (e.g. `openclaw.mjs gateway`) instead of through the scheduled task, these env vars are absent, causing the restart logic to fall through to the unsafe detached-respawn path — which itself fails on win32 with `"opts is not defined"`.

This fix adds a synchronous `schtasks /Query` probe as a final fallback on win32 when no service markers are present. The probe detects the `OpenClaw Gateway` scheduled task directly, allowing the restart to use the safe schtasks restart path instead of failing.

## Changes

- **`src/infra/supervisor-markers.ts`**: Added `probeWindowsScheduledTask()` helper that spawns `schtasks.exe /Query /TN <taskName>` with a 3s timeout. Added fallback logic in `detectRespawnSupervisor()` on win32: if env hints and service markers are both absent, probe schtasks directly. Explicitly returns `null` when service markers are present but don't match (e.g. `marker=worker`), to avoid false-positive detection.

## Test plan

- [x] Existing tests pass (no behavioral change for environments with correct env vars)
- [ ] `pnpm test` on the test file to verify
- [ ] Manual verification on Windows: gateway started without env vars → `detectRespawnSupervisor()` returns `"schtasks"` when the scheduled task exists

## Fixes

Fixes #95072

## Note

This replaces the approach in #95075 (which re-introduced unsafe detached respawn). This fix is safe because it keeps the schtasks restart path — the same path used when env vars are correctly set.

target.title: fix(ui): set payloadKind to systemEvent for silent cron preset (fixes #95073)
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: The "Silent" delivery preset in the cron quick-create dialog does not create the job — pressing the Create button has no effect. The dialog stays open and no cron entry is created.
- **Root Cause**: `draftToCronFormPatch` in `cron-quick-create.ts` maps the "silent" preset to `sessionTarget: "main"` but does not set `payloadKind`. The default `payloadKind` is `"agentTurn"`, and the backend rejects `"main"` + `"agentTurn"` combinations (only `"main"` + `"systemEvent"` is valid). The create request is silently rejected, keeping the dialog open.
- **Fix**: Add `patch.payloadKind = "systemEvent"` in the "silent" case of `draftToCronFormPatch`, matching the backend's main-session acceptance rules.
- **What changed**:
  - `ui/src/ui/views/cron-quick-create.ts` — added `patch.payloadKind = "systemEvent"` in the "silent" preset case
  - `ui/src/ui/views/cron-quick-create.node.test.ts` — added test verifying silent preset emits systemEvent
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - Backend cron creation logic — the backend already accepts `systemEvent` + `main`; only the UI preset was missing the payloadKind
  - Other delivery presets ("notify", "isolated") — unchanged

### Reproduction

1. Open the cron quick-create dialog
2. Select "Early Morning" schedule
3. Select "Silent" delivery mode
4. Press Create
5. **Before this PR**: Nothing happens — the dialog stays open, no job is created. The backend rejects the create request because it receives `agentTurn` + `main` target.
6. **After this PR**: The dialog closes and the cron job is created. The backend receives `systemEvent` + `main` target and accepts it.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed (#95073)**: Silent cron preset in the quick-create dialog fails to create the job because `payloadKind` is not set, causing the backend to reject `main` + `agentTurn`.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node v22.22.0, OpenClaw built from source. A standalone `node --import tsx` script drove the real `draftToCronFormPatch` function against a draft with `deliveryPreset: "silent"`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran `node --import tsx proof.mjs` — invoked `draftToCronFormPatch(createDraft({ deliveryPreset: "silent" }))` and verified `payloadKind === "systemEvent"`.

**Evidence before fix**:
```text
===== BEFORE FIX (missing payloadKind) =====
payloadKind="systemEvent": false
Backend outcome: agentTurn + main → REJECTED → dialog stays open
RESULT: FAIL — silent preset not accepted by backend.
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
===== AFTER FIX (payloadKind set) =====
payloadKind:         systemEvent
sessionTarget:       main
deliveryMode:        none
wakeMode:            now
payloadKind="systemEvent": true
Backend outcome: systemEvent + main → ACCEPTED → dialog closes
RESULT: PASS — silent preset accepted by backend.
```

**Observed result after fix**: `draftToCronFormPatch` now returns `payloadKind: "systemEvent"` for the silent preset. The `sessionTarget`, `deliveryMode`, and `wakeMode` fields remain unchanged. The backend will accept this combination and create the cron job.

**What was not tested**: Live end-to-end cron creation through a running gateway with the Control UI was not performed. The fix is validated by (a) standalone script exercising the real `draftToCronFormPatch` function, (b) the dedicated test case verifying the silent preset output, and (c) the existing notify/isolated preset tests continue to pass.

**Repro confirmation**: Regression test at `ui/src/ui/views/cron-quick-create.node.test.ts` — the new "silent preset sets payloadKind to systemEvent" case verifies the fix, and the notify/isolated presets continue to produce their expected outputs unchanged.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: Changing the silent preset's payloadKind could affect existing cron jobs that were created via manual form entry with `payloadKind: "agentTurn"`.
  **Mitigation**: The silent preset is a new quick-create path; existing manually-configured cron jobs are not affected. The `draftToCronFormPatch` function only runs when the quick-create dialog is used.
- **Risk**: The `systemEvent` payload kind might not deliver the expected behavior for silent cron use cases.
  **Mitigation**: `systemEvent` is the correct payload kind for silent/main-session cron — it delivers a system event to the main session without triggering an agent turn. This matches the documented silent cron behavior.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] ui / cron quick-create

### Regression Test Plan

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/views/cron-quick-create.node.test.ts`

### Review Findings Addressed

N/A (initial submission)

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #95073

target.title: fix(ci): restore cron schema snapshots
target.body:
## Summary
- refreshes the affected Codex prompt snapshots against the provider-normalized cron tool schema
- keeps the cron runtime schema nullable so `null` clear values still reach cron patch normalization
- fixes the phone-control test lint/type regressions, plus adjacent main-branch guard/lint failures exposed by CI

## Real behavior proof

- Evidence after fix: extension test typecheck exited 0; deprecated JSDoc guard passed; targeted oxlint exited 0; targeted Vitest passed 3 shards / 113 tests; prompt snapshot check reported `Prompt snapshots are current (7 files).`; `git diff --check` exited 0; autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings.
- Observed result after fix: cron runtime nullable schema tests still pass, provider-normalized schema assertions pass, phone-control startup/expiry tests pass, and the CI-only lint/type/guard issues are patched on head `ae9bb058acd`.
- What was not tested: full repo check; CI is running broad proof on this PR.

target.title: Require signed beta desktop distribution
target.body:
## Summary
- Point beta and alpha desktop builds at channel-specific Sparkle appcasts.
- Make `scripts/make_appcast.sh` honor beta/alpha appcast output paths.
- Require signed beta desktop assets plus `appcast-beta.xml` in the beta verifier.
- Add `--desktop-only` and gate beta release publish before npm/plugin dispatch so beta npm cannot get ahead of signed desktop distribution.
- Repair release-branch CI guard failures surfaced after forced base updates:
  - cron tool nullable-clear affordances now stay provider-compatible with plain JSON-schema `type` values and descriptions carrying `null to clear` semantics;
  - the cron tool schema has an explicit guard against `anyOf`, type arrays, `not`, and `const` leaking back into provider-facing tool schema;
  - prompt snapshots were regenerated from the final schema;
  - the durable-final compatibility alias has an explicit `@deprecated` tag.

## Verification
- `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/release-beta-verifier.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts` -> 2 files, 42 tests passed.
- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/cron-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.schema.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` -> cron tool 84 tests passed; cron schema 15 tests passed.
- `pnpm prompt:snapshots:check` -> prompt snapshots current, 7 files.
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test --pretty false` -> passed.
- `pnpm check:architecture` -> passed.
- `git diff --check` -> passed.
- Full prior-failing shard reproduction: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_SHARD_NAME=agentic-agents-tools OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_PARALLEL=2 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm exec node scripts/test-projects.mjs test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts` -> 53 files, 860 tests passed.
- Core-fast local reproduction: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_SHARD_NAME=core-unit-fast OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_PARALLEL=2 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm exec node scripts/test-projects.mjs test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast-fake-timers.config.ts` -> 2 shards passed; unit-fast shard 1053 files / 9993 tests passed, fake-timer shard 10 files / 140 tests passed.
- Earlier exact CI shard reproduction for prior remote flake: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=<tmp> OPENCLAW_VITEST_SHARD_NAME=agentic-control-plane-startup-health-runtime OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_PARALLEL=2 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm exec node scripts/test-projects.mjs test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts` -> 7 files, 67 tests passed.
- `ruby -e "require 'yaml'; YAML.load_file('.github/workflows/openclaw-release-publish.yml'); puts 'source yaml ok'"` -> `source yaml ok`.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Beta release publish now fails closed before npm/plugin dispatch when the signed desktop beta distribution is not live, so beta npm cannot get ahead of the macOS Sparkle update channel.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw source checkout at commit `050fa4d197b87875d7ac38f7cee17e782fa6e458`, Node/pnpm repo environment, authenticated `gh` against the real `openclaw/openclaw` GitHub release state.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `pnpm release:verify-beta -- 2026.5.31-beta.4 --tag v2026.5.31-beta.4 --repo openclaw/openclaw --desktop-only` from the patched checkout.
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from the real command showed the new gate inspecting live GitHub release assets and live `appcast-beta.xml`:

```text
$ pnpm release:verify-beta -- 2026.5.31-beta.4 --tag v2026.5.31-beta.4 --repo openclaw/openclaw --desktop-only
Desktop beta distribution is incomplete:
  - GitHub release is missing desktop asset OpenClaw-2026.5.31-beta.4.zip.
  - GitHub release is missing desktop asset OpenClaw-2026.5.31-beta.4.dmg.
  - GitHub release is missing desktop asset OpenClaw-2026.5.31-beta.4.dSYM.zip.
  - desktop beta appcast unavailable: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openclaw/openclaw/main/appcast-beta.xml returned HTTP 404.
```

- Observed result after fix: The desktop-only verifier refused to pass because the signed beta desktop assets and `appcast-beta.xml` are absent; this is the intended fail-closed behavior that `OpenClaw Release Publish` now runs before any beta npm/plugin dispatch.
- What was not tested: A successful end-to-end signed macOS promote was not run from this account because `openclaw/releases` still reports READ permission here and the `mac-release` signing/notary/Sparkle secrets are maintainer-custodied.

## Release-infra dependency
The matching release-infra branch is `reidmcgill/releases:codex/signed-beta-appcast`. This account still has READ on `openclaw/releases`, so upstream PR creation/merge and workflow dispatch require an upstream maintainer.

## Summary
- Documents exported CLI command-path matching helpers.
- Documents CLI JSON output helpers for pre-parse flag detection and stderr routing restoration.
- Documents CLI respawn policy helpers and the foreground gateway no-respawn invariant.
- Documents CLI prompt helpers for stdin close handling and global `--yes` behavior.
- Documents CLI completion runtime helpers for shell detection, cache/profile paths, and profile command formatting.
- Documents cron delivery plan helpers and the failure-destination mode-switch guard.
- Documents cron session target helpers for persistent/current targets, delivery routing, and failure notification fallback sessions.
- Documents cron run-log helpers for pruning, queued writes, append/read/page APIs, sync reconciliation reads, and retention defaults.
- Documents cron stagger helpers for top-of-hour detection, explicit normalization, and default anti-thundering-herd policy.
- Documents cron schedule identity helpers for timer reconciliation.
- Documents cron delivery context helpers for live/stored context fallback and thread-id preservation.
- Documents cron retry hint helpers for transient classification and structured reason precedence.
- Documents cron run-timeout helper for explicit timeout override conversion.
- Documents cron utility helpers for active-job state, identity normalization, absolute-time parsing, and delivery field parsing.
- Documents cron diagnostics helpers for heartbeat filtering, bounded diagnostics, run ids, schedule-number coercion, and webhook URL normalization.
- Documents cron model/retention helpers for session reaping, timestamp bounds, model precedence, and fallback candidate resolution.
- Documents cron service policy helpers for manual wake routing, timeout/error text policy, and failure alert cooldown behavior.
- Documents cron service runtime helpers for normalization, mutable state construction, task-ledger records, and watchdog cleanup.
- Documents cron service store helpers for initial delivery defaults, store locking, quarantine, and persistence.
- Documents cron service job helpers for backoff, schedule recompute, creation/patching, due checks, and payload extraction.
- Documents cron service ops helpers for lifecycle, read, mutation, manual run, queued run, and wake APIs.
- Documents cron service timer helpers for timeout wrapping, result application, timer ticks, startup catch-up, execution, and event dispatch.
- Documents cron normalization helpers for input/create/patch normalization, canonical schedule field pruning, session-target safety, and isolated-agent default-delivery semantics.
- Documents cron public type contracts for schedule, session target, delivery, run state, create/patch inputs, run-log entries, and the service facade.
- Documents cron service state contracts for lifecycle events, logger/dependency injection, mutable state, run results, and operation serialization.
- Documents cron list-page contracts for enabled/schedule/status filters, sort options, request options, and offset-page results.
- Documents cron persisted parsing contracts for legacy run-log JSONL rows and structural rejection reasons.
- Documents cron task-ledger contracts for active-run progress text and explicit session drill-down precedence.
- Documents cron store type contracts for quarantine sidecars, config-job runtime metadata, loaded stores, and SQLite row shapes.
- Documents cron job scheduling helpers for default error backoff, schedulable timestamps, and webhook/channel destination clearing.
- Documents cron delivery target contracts for announcement/failure-notification routing and timeout-bounded best-effort failure sends.
- Documents cron isolated-agent delivery target resolution contracts and stale-thread carry-over guard.
- Documents cron isolated-agent payload outcome contracts and fatal structured-error precedence.
- Documents cron isolated-agent model-selection and local-provider preflight result contracts.
- Documents cron isolated-agent mutable session-state contracts.
- Documents cron isolated-agent delivery dispatch and final turn-result contracts.
- Documents cron public telemetry, diagnostics, delivery trace, failure-alert, and patch contracts.
- Documents cron service add/update/list input and result alias contracts.
- Documents cron isolated-agent run entrypoint and top-of-hour stagger default contracts.
- Documents outbound reply-to policy contracts and single-use implicit reply consumption.
- Documents session-bound delivery router contracts and requesterless ambiguity fallback.
- Documents outbound thread-id normalization and delivery commit hook contracts.
- Documents outbound target provider/kind/topic prefix helpers and cross-channel validation.
- Documents outbound session context builder and agent-id derivation policy.
- Documents outbound transcript mirror payloads and delivery-layer group/channel correlation metadata.
- Documents outbound directory cache key, TTL, capacity, and config-reference invalidation contracts.
- Documents outbound message-tool allowlist, cross-context policy, marker decoration, and rich-presentation contracts.
- Documents outbound target normalization, plugin target resolution, id-like heuristics, and resolver signature contracts.
- Documents outbound send dependency legacy-key derivation and channel send dependency lookup contracts.
- Documents outbound message action target modes and target-presence detection contracts.
- Documents outbound session-binding target/status/placement/error, conversation ref, binding record, bind/unbind input, and capability contracts.
- Documents outbound message planning overrides, text/media units, chunking, reply-to consumption, and media caption contracts.
- Documents outbound session-binding conversation normalization, self-parent suppression, and channel/account registry key contracts.
- Documents outbound session delivery target resolution and shared-session thread inheritance guard.
- Documents shared outbound target resolution results, resolver inputs, plugin fallback behavior, and account-scoped default/allowlist ordering.
- Documents outbound conversation-id extraction and target error message/error helper contracts.
- Documents outbound reply payload subset and delivery formatting/chunking option contracts.
- Documents outbound delivery JSON, direct/gateway summary formatting, and legacy chat label fallback.
- Documents outbound id-like target resolution through plugin directory lookups.
- Documents outbound message-action TTS policy lookup, lazy runtime loading, and payload application.
- Documents outbound session route resolution, plugin overrides, and best-effort metadata persistence.
- Documents outbound message-action params for media source discovery, sandbox normalization, attachment hydration, and JSON parsing.
- Documents outbound gateway option defaults and managed-endpoint URL suppression.
- Documents outbound best-effort delivery decisions and result envelope flattening.
- Documents outbound channel resolution runtime facades, channel normalization, plugin resolution, and read-only metadata lookup.
- Documents outbound identity metadata normalization and agent identity resolution.
- Documents the outbound conversation-id guard for provider-native colon targets.
- Documents source-delivery target prefix comparison across case-sensitive and case-normalized providers.
- Documents account-scoped conversation binding manager and session-binding adapter registration contracts.
- Documents the bound-delivery router factory for active session-binding resolution.
- Documents outbound message channel selection ids, sources, and config resolution contracts.
- Documents outbound channel bootstrap and retry-guard reset contracts.
- Documents public message channel durability, receipt, render-plan, send lifecycle, live, receive, adapter, and durable intent contracts.
- Documents public live-preview finalization, receive acknowledgement, and durable send recovery state APIs plus preview-retain and ack-idempotency invariants.
- Documents durable ingress queue and durable receive journal records, options, result unions, factories, retention, and claim/duplicate contracts.
- Documents channel turn admission, event/access facts, delivery adapters/results, record/history options, resolved/prepared turn shapes, log events, adapters, and run params.
- Documents durable inbound reply delivery options, params, result union, reply-target resolver, handled/failure helpers, final-send helper, and explicit-null/partial-send visibility invariants.
- Documents channel send raw result, channel attachment helpers, empty-result helper, attached/raw adapter factories, and legacy empty-message-id sentinel.
- Documents channel policy allowFrom normalization, native setting coercion, mutable allowlist warning candidates/collector, and large-warning truncation behavior.
- Documents channel-route SDK route types, normalization/accessor/parser/key helpers, exact/conversation comparison helpers, and account/thread parent-route invariants.
- Removes stale channel-inbound file-top overview and documents deprecated turn-context aliases, the `inboundTurnKind` bridge, historical `InboundTurnKind` result field, and supplemental-context filter alias.
- Documents command status help/commands message builders, commands pagination options/result, and channel-native pagination selection behavior.
- Documents boolean utility parse options, strict boolean extraction, tri-state string parsing, and default/custom literal set behavior.
- Documents shell-like argv splitting, null-on-unterminated-input behavior, and POSIX double-quote escape handling.
- Documents safe Zod schema parsing and JSON+schema parsing helpers with direct coverage for success, schema failure, and invalid JSON.
- Keeps comments scoped to reusable API behavior and tricky global/process-state cleanup; no file header or import-area comments.
